stem: For a ladder with \( x^{2}+y^{2}=L^{2} \), \( x\frac{dx}{dt}+y\frac{dy}{dt}=0 \). If \( x=[[x]] \), \( y=[[y]] \), and \( \frac{dx}{dt}=[[dxdt]] \), find \( \frac{dy}{dt} \).

A person of height \( [[h]] \) ft walks directly away from a lamppost of height \( [[H]] \) ft at a rate \( \frac{dx}{dt}=[[dxdt]] \) ft/s, where \( x \) is the person's distance from the pole. If \( s \) is the length of the person's shadow, similar triangles give \( \dfrac{[[H]]}{x+s}=\dfrac{[[h]]}{s} \). At what rate is the shadow length \( \dfrac{ds}{dt} \) increasing?
solution: Solving the proportion gives \( s=\dfrac{[[h]]}{[[H]]-[[h]]}x \), so \( \dfrac{ds}{dt}=\dfrac{[[h]]}{[[H]]-[[h]]}\dfrac{dx}{dt}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of heights.
This omits the height factor \( h \) in the numerator.
This assumes the shadow grows at the same rate the person walks.
This uses the lamppost height \( H \) instead of \( H-h \) in the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: An object of height \( [[h]] \) m sits atop a pole. An observer at ground level, horizontal distance \( x \) from the pole's base, walks away at \( \frac{dx}{dt}=[[dxdt]] \) m/s. Let \( \theta \) be the angle of elevation from the observer to the top of the object, so \( \tan\theta=\dfrac{[[h]]}{x} \). At the moment \( x=[[x]] \), what is \( \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} \)?
solution: Differentiating \( \tan\theta=\dfrac{[[h]]}{x} \) implicitly, \( \sec^{2}\theta\,\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}=-\dfrac{[[h]]}{x^{2}}\dfrac{dx}{dt} \), and since \( \sec^{2}\theta=1+\dfrac{[[h]]^{2}}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{x^{2}+[[h]]^{2}}{x^{2}} \), this simplifies to \( \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}=-\dfrac{[[h]]\,dx/dt}{x^{2}+[[h]]^{2}}=[[answer]] \) at \( x=[[x]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign; \( \theta \) decreases as the observer walks away.
This omits the factor of \( h \) in the numerator.
This omits the \( h^{2} \) term in the denominator.
This uses \( x \) instead of \( x^{2}+h^{2} \) in the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Water drains from an inverted conical tank of height \( [[H]] \) m and top radius \( [[R]] \) m. By similar triangles, the radius of the water's surface at height \( h \) is \( r=\dfrac{[[R]]}{[[H]]}h \), so the volume is \( V=\dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^{2}h \). If \( \dfrac{dV}{dt}=-[[k]]\pi \) m\(^3\)/min and the water height is currently \( h=[[h]] \) m, find \( \dfrac{dh}{dt} \).
solution: \( V=\dfrac{\pi}{3}\left(\dfrac{[[R]]}{[[H]]}\right)^{2}h^{3} \), so \( \dfrac{dV}{dt}=\pi\left(\dfrac{[[R]]}{[[H]]}\right)^{2}h^{2}\dfrac{dh}{dt} \). Setting this equal to \( -[[k]]\pi \) and solving at \( h=[[h]] \) gives \( \dfrac{dh}{dt}=-\dfrac{[[k]]([[H]])^{2}}{([[R]])^{2}([[h]])^{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign; the water level is falling.
This inverts the ratio \( R/H \), using \( R^2/H^2 \) in the wrong place.
This omits the tank's radius-to-height ratio entirely.
This omits a factor of \( h \) (uses \( h^{1} \) instead of \( h^{2} \)).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Two cars leave an intersection along perpendicular roads. At a certain moment, car A is \( [[x]] \) mi east of the intersection and moving away at \( \frac{dx}{dt}=[[dxdt]] \) mi/h, while car B is \( [[y]] \) mi north of the intersection and moving away at \( \frac{dy}{dt}=[[dydt]] \) mi/h. If \( D \) is the distance between the cars, find \( \dfrac{dD}{dt} \) at this moment.
distractor_rationales: This simply adds the two speeds, ignoring the geometry entirely.
This has the wrong sign on the second term.
This uses \( x+y \) instead of \( \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \) in the denominator.
This reports the current distance \( D \) itself, not its rate of change.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
